title: Three sins against physics by an exaggerated quantum information perspective
abstract: I point out three ways in which the perspective of quantum information may lead to distorted claims about physics: forgetting that light does not need to be quantised to show coherence; ignoring the generators of unitary evolutions; and approaching the discovery of nature as a fight against an adversary.
summary: This paper critiques the 'quantum information perspective' for potentially distorting our understanding of physics. It argues that focusing too heavily on gates, ignoring Hamiltonians, and treating nature as an adversary can lead to incorrect physical conclusions. The work serves as a foundational reminder to ground quantum information theory in underlying physical reality.
main_problem: The author addresses how an over-reliance on the quantum information perspective can lead to physical distortions, specifically by over-abstracting reality through gates, ignoring Hamiltonians, and adopting an adversarial view of nature.
main_result: The paper identifies three 'sins': confusing classical mode coherence with quantum state coherence, focusing on unitary gates while ignoring their underlying Hamiltonians, and treating physical discovery as an adversarial game.
method: The author uses conceptual and philosophical analysis of quantum information frameworks, such as resource theories, nonlocal games, and quantum circuits, contrasted with fundamental principles of optics and quantum mechanics.
model_or_system: The discussion covers optical systems (linear and non-linear interferometry, Maxwell's equations), quantum information models (qubits, Clifford gates, Fock space), and the relationship between unitary evolutions and Hamiltonians.
key_observables: First-order coherence (g1), second-order coherence (g2), photon counting statistics, and Bell nonlocality.
important_parameters: Single-photon regime (energy thresholds), Clifford operations, and the distinction between adversarial and non-adversarial settings.
main_assumptions: The author critiques the assumption that an adversarial 'game' framework is the most appropriate way to understand the ontology of quantum mechanics.
paper_structure: The paper is structured around three main arguments (the 'sins'), followed by a concluding reflection on the philosophical implications of the adversarial view in experimental physics.
why_interesting: It provides a critical perspective on the foundational assumptions used in quantum optics and quantum information, specifically regarding the physical reality of quantum resources versus classical wave phenomena.
analysis_basis: full PDF text, analyzed in chunks
